title: Read the Failure Cause in systemctl Status
hook: The status page often tells you the failed startup step before you open every log.
problem: A systemd service is failed and you need the active state, exit status, unit paths, and the most recent failure lines in one read-only check.
starting_state: systemctl --failed shows app-worker.service in failed state after several restart attempts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemctl prints service metadata, process exit status, and recent journal lines.
when_to_use: Use first when a specific service is failed and you need a fast summary before deeper journal or unit-file inspection.
when_not_to_use: Do not stop at status when the failure is intermittent, old, or missing context; follow up with journalctl and systemctl show.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A failed active state, Result value, ExecStart exit status, and recent lines showing the failing startup step.

shorts.title: Start with the failed service status.
shorts.voiceover: When a unit is failed, status gives the state, result, exit code, and the newest journal lines in one screen.
shorts.caption: Status first, then deeper logs.

linkedin.question: When a service fails, do you read systemctl status before opening the full journal?
linkedin.cta: Use status as the first evidence snapshot.

youtube.description: Use systemctl status with --no-pager and a line cap to inspect a failed service without changing it.

experiments.variant_a: Status often names the failed startup step.
experiments.variant_b: One screen before full log digging.

title: Print the Exact systemd Exit Fields
hook: Turn a noisy service failure into four fields you can paste into an incident note.
problem: Full status output is useful for humans, but incident notes and scripts need compact fields like Result, ExecMainStatus, and restart count.
starting_state: A service has failed and you need machine-readable failure metadata from systemd.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemctl show prints selected unit properties.
when_to_use: Use when documenting a failure, comparing before and after recovery, or writing a runbook check.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat numeric status values as self-explanatory; pair them with status or journal lines.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Result, ExecMainCode, ExecMainStatus, and NRestarts property lines.

shorts.title: Print compact failure fields.
shorts.voiceover: For incident notes, ask systemd for the exact failure fields instead of copying a whole status page.
shorts.caption: Compact evidence: systemctl show.

linkedin.question: Which systemd fields do you capture in incident notes?
linkedin.cta: Pair compact fields with the log line that explains them.

youtube.description: Use systemctl show with selected properties to capture compact systemd failure metadata.

experiments.variant_a: Turn service failure into four fields.
experiments.variant_b: Cleaner incident notes for failed units.

title: Read Warning and Error Logs for One Failed Unit
hook: Filter a failed unit's journal to the lines most likely to explain the stop.
problem: A unit journal contains startup chatter, retries, and supervisor messages, and you need the warning-or-worse lines first.
starting_state: app-worker.service failed during the current boot and its full journal includes both normal and severe lines.
what_changed: Nothing changes. journalctl prints warning, error, critical, alert, and emergency entries for the unit.
when_to_use: Use after status when you need the likely failure lines without scrolling through every normal startup message.
when_not_to_use: Do not use severity filtering if the app logs important failures at info level; check the unfiltered unit journal too.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Current-boot warning-or-worse journal lines for the failed service.

shorts.title: Filter the failed unit journal.
shorts.voiceover: After status, filter the current boot journal to warning and worse. It cuts noise while keeping the likely failure clues.
shorts.caption: Current boot, warning and worse.

linkedin.question: Do your services log real failures at warning or error level consistently?
linkedin.cta: Use severity filtering as a fast first pass, then verify with full logs.

youtube.description: Use journalctl with a unit, current boot, and priority range to inspect severe lines from a failed service.

experiments.variant_b: Warning and worse for one failed unit.

title: Build a Restart Loop Timeline
hook: Restart loops make more sense when you line up starts, failures, and counters.
problem: A service keeps restarting and you need to separate the first application failure from later supervisor retries.
command: journalctl -u app-worker -b --no-pager -o short-iso | grep -E 'Started|Failed|Scheduled restart|Main process exited'
starting_state: The service has failed repeatedly in the current boot and systemd has recorded restart attempts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The pipeline prints a compact timeline of start, failure, and restart-counter lines.
when_to_use: Use when Restart=on-failure is hiding the first useful failure under repeated retries.
when_not_to_use: Do not use this as the only diagnosis; read the adjacent app log lines around the first failure.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Timestamped service lifecycle lines showing starts, main-process exits, failed results, and scheduled restarts.

commands: journalctl -u app-worker -b --no-pager -o short-iso | grep -E 'Started|Failed|Scheduled restart|Main process exited'

shorts.title: Make the restart loop visible.
shorts.voiceover: When systemd retries a service, line up the starts, exits, failures, and restart counters before blaming the latest line.
shorts.caption: Restart loops need a timeline.

linkedin.question: When a service is flapping, how do you find the first useful failure?
linkedin.cta: Build the restart timeline before changing unit settings.

youtube.description: Use journalctl and grep to build a compact timeline for a failing systemd restart loop.

experiments.variant_b: Restart loops hide the first clue.

title: Inspect the Unit File and Drop-ins Together
hook: The bug may be in an override file, not the main unit.
problem: A service definition looks correct in the main unit, but a drop-in override may be changing the user, environment, or command.
starting_state: A failed service has at least one systemd drop-in override.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemctl prints the effective unit file fragments and drop-ins in order.
when_to_use: Use when status mentions a drop-in, a service changed after deployment, or the failure points at user, path, environment, or ExecStart.
when_not_to_use: Do not edit from memory after reading this; make a deliberate unit or drop-in change and reload systemd when applying a fix.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: The main service unit followed by drop-in override contents.

shorts.title: Check drop-ins before editing.
shorts.voiceover: If status shows a drop-in, inspect the unit and override together. The effective config may not be in the file you expected.
shorts.caption: systemctl cat shows unit fragments.

linkedin.question: How often do systemd drop-ins explain a service that suddenly changed behavior?
linkedin.cta: Inspect the effective unit before editing.

youtube.description: Use systemctl cat to inspect a service unit and its drop-in overrides together.

experiments.variant_a: The bug may live in the override.

title: Print Runtime Paths and User From systemd
hook: Confirm the user, working directory, env file, and ExecStart systemd is actually using.
problem: A service failure points at credentials or paths, and you need systemd's resolved unit properties without reading multiple files manually.
starting_state: A failed service has unit files, an environment file, an ExecStart command, and a drop-in override.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemctl prints selected runtime configuration properties.
when_to_use: Use when a service fails at USER, CHDIR, EXEC, environment-file loading, or after a deploy that changed paths.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume these values prove the files exist or permissions are correct; follow up with ls, stat, getent, or namei as needed.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Selected unit properties showing fragment paths, drop-ins, environment files, ExecStart, User, and WorkingDirectory.

shorts.title: Ask systemd what it will run.
shorts.voiceover: When a service fails on user or path setup, print the resolved properties. It shows the user, working directory, env files, and command.
shorts.caption: Resolved service config, read-only.

linkedin.question: When a service fails at USER, EXEC, or CHDIR, which property do you check first?
linkedin.cta: Use systemctl show before chasing the wrong file.

youtube.description: Use systemctl show to print the resolved user, working directory, environment files, ExecStart, and unit paths for a failed service.

experiments.variant_a: Ask systemd what it will run.

title: Check Failed Dependencies for a Service
hook: Sometimes the service is only the messenger for a failed dependency.
problem: A service fails after startup, but the real blocker may be a dependent unit such as Redis, networking, a mount, or a socket.
starting_state: app-worker.service depends on other systemd units and at least one dependency is failed.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemctl prints failed dependencies for the requested unit.
when_to_use: Use when a service failure mentions connection refused, missing mounts, network targets, sockets, or required local services.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every dependency failure is causal; inspect the dependency logs and timeline.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A dependency tree containing failed units under the target service.

shorts.title: Check failed dependencies.
shorts.voiceover: A failed app service may only be reporting that Redis, a mount, or networking is broken. Ask systemd for failed dependencies.
shorts.caption: The dependency may be the cause.

linkedin.question: How often is a failed service actually a failed dependency?
linkedin.cta: Check the dependency tree before restarting the app repeatedly.

youtube.description: Use systemctl list-dependencies with --failed to find failed units under a broken service.

experiments.variant_a: The service may be the messenger.

title: Reset Failed State After Capturing Evidence
hook: Clear the red failed state only after you have captured the evidence.
problem: systemctl --failed still shows an old service failure after the issue was fixed, and you need to clear the recorded failed state deliberately.
starting_state: You have already captured status and journal evidence for app-worker.service and are ready to clear its failed marker.
what_changed: systemd clears the recorded failed state for the service, so future --failed output reflects new failures.
when_to_use: Use after documenting or fixing a failure when the failed state itself is stale noise.
when_not_to_use: Do not run it before collecting status and journal evidence, and do not confuse clearing state with fixing the service.
undo: There is no meaningful undo for clearing the marker; use saved logs or incident notes for prior evidence.
expected_output: Either no output or a short confirmation that the failed state was reset.

shorts.title: Reset failed only after evidence.
shorts.voiceover: reset-failed clears the marker; it does not fix the cause. Capture status and logs first, then clear stale failure state.
shorts.caption: Clear state after capture.

linkedin.question: Do your runbooks capture evidence before clearing systemd failed state?
linkedin.cta: Treat reset-failed as cleanup, not diagnosis.

youtube.description: Use systemctl reset-failed only after capturing service failure evidence.

title: Find the First Failure Line for One Unit
hook: The first failure line is often more useful than the last restart message.
problem: A failed service has repeated restart noise, and you need the earliest current-boot line that names an error or failed startup step.
starting_state: app-worker.service has failed more than once during the current boot and its journal includes both app errors and systemd failure messages.
what_changed: Nothing changes. journalctl prints the current-boot unit log and grep stops at the first matching failure line.
when_to_use: Use when restart loops bury the original clue under repeated systemd retry messages.
when_not_to_use: Do not use this as a complete log review; it intentionally stops at the first matching line and can miss later, more specific evidence.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: The earliest current-boot app error, failed step, or status line for the unit.

shorts.title: Find the first failure line.
shorts.voiceover: Restart loops repeat the same failure. Search the current boot journal and stop at the first error-like line.
shorts.caption: First clue before repeated retries.

linkedin.question: When a service restarts repeatedly, do you inspect the first failure or the latest one first?
linkedin.cta: Find the first failure line before tuning restart behavior.

youtube.description: Use journalctl and grep -m1 to find the first current-boot failure clue for a systemd unit.

experiments.variant_a: The first failure line beats retry noise.
experiments.variant_b: Stop at the first useful clue.

title: Compare Failure Output With the Effective Unit
hook: Put the failed step next to the unit config that created it.
problem: systemctl status names a failed startup step, but you need to connect that symptom to the effective unit and drop-in configuration.
command: systemctl status app-worker --no-pager --lines=50 && systemctl cat app-worker
starting_state: app-worker.service is failed and status reports a USER startup failure while the unit has a drop-in override.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The commands print failure evidence followed by the unit fragments systemd loaded.
when_to_use: Use when status mentions USER, CHDIR, EXEC, environment files, or a drop-in and you need the config beside the failure evidence.
when_not_to_use: Do not edit the service until you know whether the main unit or a drop-in owns the setting you plan to change.
undo: No undo needed because the commands are read-only.
expected_output: A failed status page followed by the main unit file and drop-in override that changes the service user.

shorts.title: Put failure next to config.
shorts.voiceover: When status names a failed step, print the effective unit right after it. The override often explains the symptom.
shorts.caption: Evidence first, config beside it.

linkedin.question: Do you compare systemd status output with the effective unit before editing overrides?
linkedin.cta: Keep the symptom and loaded config in the same incident note.

youtube.description: Compare systemctl status with systemctl cat to connect a failed startup step to the effective unit configuration.

experiments.variant_b: The override may explain the failure.
